does: This paper introduces LASH, a black-box jailbreaking framework that synthesizes multiple existing attack methods into a single adaptive prompt for each target request. Rather than committing to one attack family, LASH maintains a pool of diverse base attacks, searches over subsets and mixture weights of these seed prompts, and uses a genetic optimizer with black-box feedback to refine compositions. The approach combines keyword-based refusal detection with LLM-judge evaluation in a two-stage fitness function. Experiments on JailbreakBench demonstrate that adaptive hybridization of complementary attack strategies achieves superior performance compared to individual attack methods across different target models and harm categories.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of automated jailbreak attacks against aligned language models, extending prior research that has explored single attack families including refinement loops, tree search strategies, mutation spaces, and strategy libraries. It responds to an empirical observation that no single attack method consistently dominates across all models and harm categories, suggesting that different approaches have complementary strengths. The paper connects to meta-learning and algorithm selection traditions by treating attack generation as a composition problem over diverse base methods. It also engages with black-box optimization techniques and refusal detection methods used in prior safety evaluation work, synthesizing these into an integrated framework for prompt engineering at scale.
enables: This work opens directions for studying hybrid and ensemble approaches to adversarial attacks on language models, potentially inspiring defenses that account for compositional attack strategies. The adaptive weighting mechanism could extend to other domains where multiple base methods exist but no single approach dominates. The framework raises questions about the theoretical properties of composite attacks and whether defenses can be designed to be robust against arbitrary mixtures of attack families. Future work might explore how such hybridization techniques scale to larger seed pools, how to discover new complementary attack families, and whether similar adaptive composition principles apply to attacking other AI safety properties beyond jailbreaking.
